module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
sublecture:
2.1.2 Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844 - Part 2
, Incomplete unit
content: So what is the central economic fact that Marx wants us to start with. The central economic fact for Marx, and this is the foundation of his work. The central economic fact is that the worker grows poorer, the more wealth she creates. Her labour becomes cheaper as commodities increase as the value of things rise, the value of humans fall. So labour produces not just commodities, it produces the worker itself becomes a commodity. So the concept that Marks wants us to understand, he calls it objectification of labour. What does objectification of labour mean? It means that the product of labour stands outside the labour as a power independent of the producer, the labourer, so labour becomes embodied in an object. This is called the objectification of labour. It's called the realization of labour. Now, this realization of labour is essential and it's an inherent part of labour. Now the problem becomes when this objectification that is the realization of labour into this object that labour has produced. This is so far so good. The problem occurs according to Marx when this objectification becomes estrangement under the capitalist economic system. So what is Mark saying? Okay, this is a slightly sort of convoluted point, so. Let me try my best. So what Marx is saying is under the economic conditions of modern capitalism, the realization of labour that is the objectification of labour. This product that has been, that has been created by labour, it appears as a loss of realization for the workers. So instead of empowerment, the realization of labour results in the loss of the object and a bondage to the object. It appears as estrangement, as alienation. So this might seem very obscure and you might think, well, what is the point of this? How does this relate to me and my life? And the fact that I want to be an entrepreneur, right. So I would request you to remember the Charlie Chaplin video, which was sort of a pre-work before looking at Marx. So you must have seen this Charlie Chaplin video which shows the state of workers under industrialization. They are reduced to this mindless mind-numbing routine activity that requires no intelligence or creative expression. Charlie Chaplin is just, you know, he just, his job is just to kind of bang something or, you know, that's it. That's his entire job the whole day. All he does is like this. And then at the end of it, some product is created and in the video they don't even show us. This repetitive activity that these hundreds of workers are doing including Charlie Chaplin, they don't even show us what is the final product. Right. So that is estrangement. So Charlie Chaplin and the other workers, they are creating something but the object that they were creating, it doesn't belong to them. In, in, in a very real sense, it belongs to the capitalist, the owner of the factory. And in another sense, it doesn't belong to them in the sense that the, that object is not an expression of their creativity. It doesn't embody who they are. So they are estranged from it. Now keep in mind the other video which we also posted, this is some social media start from China who became very famous, I think sometime back. Because she shows how she has gone back to her village and she creates these products from nature whether it's a woollen coat, you know, starting from sharing, taking care of the sheep you know, sharing the wool to curing the wool, and to actually create those you know threads, woollen threads, and then stitching it together to create this woollen coat. So these are the two opposites you know, to, to help you to understand what Marx has in mind. So on one hand you have this this Chinese social media you know, influencer and, you know, just to clarify, I am not in any way endorsing her. It's just an example to help you understand what Marx is saying. So it, it, it sort of shows how whatever that will woollen coat or whatever she creates, it has her unique stamp on it. So anyone who has seen her work anywhere in the world, if they see a coat they know that, oh, this was created by this person. So in the sense that the object of her labour, she owns the object of her labour, she can sell it but she owns it in a different way too, in a much deeper sense in that, that object. Any of us, when we look at it, we know, oh, this is her work. This is Van Gogh's work. This is some of the artist's work just by looking at the product we know that this is an expression of this human being. So the, the object of your work has your stamp on it. It is you, it is you embodied in this thing. So that is the ideal. And then in contrast is the Charlie Chaplin video where you see where he's just mindlessly doing this. At the end of it, whatever the product is, we don't even see the product in that video. That product is out of his control. He has no rights to that product. And in no way does that product reflect Charlie Chaplin, right or that worker who's doing that job. So that is essentially what Marx is saying, that appropriation leads to estrangement and alienation, you know, appropriation of the product of labour. So the worker in the factory situation whatever object is created that object the factory worker cannot take it and sell it. It doesn't belong to him. So the more the worker produces objects what happens is Marx is saying that the more those objects do not belong to him but to someone else, the capitalist. So instead of becoming richer by producing more and more, the worker actually is becoming more and more estranged from the object. The worker's role is becoming smaller and smaller and smaller. Now you see you know, we discussed Adam Smith before and you saw how division of labour and Adam Smith's perspective on what an awesome thing division of labour is. Right. And here we see the exact opposite. It's the other side of the coin so to say, where Marx is talking about how this division of labour, more and more the worker is losing any kind of expression through that object. So labour's realization appears as a loss of realization for the worker. So another quote from Marx. Marx says, "so much does objectification appear as loss of the object that the worker is robbed of the objects most necessary, not only for his life but for his work. Indeed labour itself becomes an object which she can obtain only with the greatest effort and with the most irregular interruptions". Now this last line you know, many of you might resonate with it. You know, when you think about it from Marxist's perspective, it's, it's actually quite strange. We are all searching for a job. Marx would say that makes no sense. Work is intrinsic to humans. It is my labour. How can I search for the right to labour? It is an intrinsic part of being human. Yeah, so Mark says labour itself becomes an object which she can obtain only with the greatest effort and with the most irregular interruptions. And Marx again goes back to criticizing the current political economy theorist and says that political economy doesn't recognize alienation of the worker. It assumes that it is normal for labour to be treated as a commodity where one labourer is the same as another. It assumes that it is normal for labour to be treated as a commodity rather than as a human being in need of respect and value and dignity with their work, a unique embodiment of themselves which cannot be separated from themselves. Now, Marx describes this in more detail. So basically, Marx's view is that labour in the way it should be. We use nature, human beings use nature as our material. So nature is the material on which labour can act and it also provides the means of the worker subsistence. So nature provides the material. We use that material, we produce something which reflects us, and that then that product now belongs to us, and it also gives us our means of subsistence. But Marx talks about how in the modern capitalist system as the worker produces, the object he produces from nature seizes to belong to him. With her unique individual stamp of creation, it seizes us to belong to him in the sense that whatever the price you get doesn't belong to her but also it doesn't reflect her unique individual stamp of creation. He see, she sees us to receive the benefits of the object she creates that is she loses her means of subsistence. So just to survive, you have to search for work and someone has to give you work, which to Marx is just crazy. Actually, when we think about it. It is crazy, right. So work worker becomes a servant of her object worker receives work. He receives the means of subsistence as wages. He exists only enough to maintain himself so she, she can continue to work. This is the lowest wage possible so that the worker can just exist and continue to work. So the worker is reduced to her mere basic physical survival. So she can continue and perform work. The more value she creates, the more valueless she becomes. The better the product, the more deformed the worker, the more she produces, the less she herself has to consume. Now let's look at another quote from Marx," It is true that labour produces wonderful things for the rich but for the worker, it produces privation. It produces palaces, but for the worker, hovels. It produces beauty but for the worker deformity, it replaces labour by machines. But it throws one section of the workers back to a barbarous type of labour, and it turns the other section into a machine. It produces intelligence, but for the worker stupidity, cretinism". End quote. So with this, mark sets the stage for the first form of alienation. And then he will go on in the rest of the economic and philosophical manuscripts to describe three other forms of alienation, the first form of alienation which we discussed in the subsection is it, it's essentially alienation from the product of labour which has two aspects to it. One is the product of labour. The object itself, you lose control of it. It belongs to the capitalist, so you cannot become rich because of it. That is the most obvious form of alienation. The second more subtle form of alienation is that your creativity, this product no longer expresses and embodies you as a human being and reflects your true essence. So you are alienated from this object that has been created. So while trying to understand this, just keep you know, the background in mind that Charlie Chaplin video and that video of that social influencer as to contrasting things to understand what Marx is saying.
